name: Tierra de Pinares: Portillo and Mojados Loop
name_original: Tierra de Pinares – circular desde Portillo por Mojados
description: Explore the Land of Pine Forests on this moderate 21.8-mile touring bicycle route from Portillo, featuring forests, fields, and historic sit
region_intro: Touring cycling around Portillo offers routes through a landscape characterized by the Duero river basin, extensive pine forests, and historical landmarks. The terrain generally features gentle undulations, with some routes including more significant climbs. This region provides a mix of open agricultural areas and shaded woodland paths, suitable for various cycling preferences. Best touring cycling routes around Portillo The most popular touring cycling route is Senda del Duero Loop from Tudela de Duero, a 12.0 miles (19.2 km) trail that takes 1 hour 28 minutes to complete, following the Duero river. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Tierra de Pinares: Portillo and Mojados Loop, a moderate 21.8 miles (35.1 km) path through pine forests and rural areas. Local touring cyclists also love the Portillo Castle – Hermitage loop from Portillo, a 17.8 miles (28.6 km) trail leading through historical sites and local countryside, often completed in about 2 hours 0 minutes. Touring cycling around Portillo is defined by river paths, pine woodlands, and historical architecture. The network offers options for easy rides and more challenging routes with varied elevation. The routes in Portillo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 4 reviews. More than 260 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Portillo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes around Portillo?
A: The Portillo region offers a diverse landscape for touring cyclists. You'll find routes characterized by the Duero river basin, extensive pine forests, and historical landmarks. The terrain generally features gentle undulations, with some routes including more significant climbs. Expect a mix of open agricultural areas and shaded woodland paths.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Portillo?
A: Yes, Portillo has several easy touring cycling routes perfect for beginners or families. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1175346015/senda-del-duero-loop-from-tudela-de-duero" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Senda del Duero Loop from Tudela de Duero&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 12.0-mile (19.2 km) trail that follows the Duero river, offering a gentle ride. Another easy option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31479446" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;MTB/BMX Circuit loop from Tudela de Duero&lt;/a&gt;, which is 8.9 miles (14.3 km) long with minimal elevation gain.

Q: What are some more challenging touring cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: For experienced touring cyclists looking for a challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38212265" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portillo Castle – Hermitage loop from Portillo&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 17.8-mile (28.6 km) trail that includes notable elevation changes and leads through historical sites and local countryside. Another moderate option with more climbing is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20149660" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tudela de Duero Bridge – Viewpoint of Traspinedo loop from Tudela de Duero&lt;/a&gt;, covering 26.8 miles (43.1 km) with over 400 meters of ascent.

Q: Are there many circular touring cycling routes in the Portillo area?
A: Many of the touring cycling routes around Portillo are designed as loops, making them convenient for starting and ending in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1175206258/tierra-de-pinares-portillo-and-mojados-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tierra de Pinares: Portillo and Mojados Loop&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1175346015/senda-del-duero-loop-from-tudela-de-duero" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Senda del Duero Loop from Tudela de Duero&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38212265" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portillo Castle – Hermitage loop from Portillo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What historical sites or landmarks can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: The Portillo region is rich in history. Along your rides, you can explore several notable landmarks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5838613" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portillo Castle&lt;/a&gt; is a prominent feature, and routes like the Portillo Castle – Hermitage loop pass directly by it. You might also encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5780020" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Juan Church in Mojados&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5597863" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Megeces Bridge&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4011321" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Hermitage of Traspinedo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural features define the touring cycling experience in Portillo?
A: The natural landscape of Portillo is characterized by the serene Duero river basin and extensive pine forests. Many routes, such as the Senda del Duero Loop, follow the river, offering picturesque views. You'll also find yourself cycling through shaded woodland paths and open agricultural areas, providing a varied and refreshing experience.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Portillo?
A: While specific weather conditions vary, the spring and autumn months generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for touring cycling in Portillo, avoiding the peak heat of summer and potential chill of winter. These seasons provide comfortable conditions to enjoy the region's river paths and pine forests.

Q: Is parking available near the touring cycling routes in Portillo?
A: Yes, parking is generally available in the towns and villages that serve as starting points for many routes, such as Portillo and Tudela de Duero. These locations often have public parking areas suitable for cyclists looking to access the trails.

Q: Can I access the touring cycling routes in Portillo using public transport?
A: Access to the Portillo region and its cycling routes via public transport can be limited, especially for carrying bicycles. It's advisable to check local bus or train schedules for services connecting to towns like Portillo or Tudela de Duero, and confirm their bicycle transport policies in advance.

Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in the Portillo area?
A: There are over 50 touring cycling routes available around Portillo, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels and preferences. This includes 9 easy routes, 23 moderate routes, and 19 difficult routes, ensuring there's something for everyone.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Portillo?
A: The touring cycling routes in Portillo are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.7 stars from over 4 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse scenery, which includes river paths and pine woodlands, and the well-maintained trails that cater to various cycling preferences.
tips: Of very remote origin, it is already mentioned in the Arab incursions through Castile in the 10th century, in its current structure the castle appears at the end of the 14th century and at the beginning of the 15th it was a Gothic building in the hands of the Sandoval family.

Valladolid town where we find two beautiful churches built by mixing brick and masonry.
The Church of San Juan, Mudejar from the 13th and 14th centuries, in which its beautiful tower stands out, and the doorway with a pointed horseshoe arch.
The other church, very close to the path of the Cega river, is that of Santa María, Mudejar and from the 14th century.

The bridge over the Cega River, built between 1515 and 1516, is a remarkable structure commissioned by the Archpriest of Cogeces, Juan Díaz de Mirena. It consists of a single limestone semicircular arch and is twelve meters long. In the lower part, it has buttresses, and its railing is decorated with large semicircular stones. Inside the arch, fragments of the old wooden framework used during its construction are preserved.

faqs.question: Is this a loop trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, the "Tierra de Pinares – circular desde Portillo por Mojados" is a circular route, meaning you will finish at your starting point in Portillo.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is generally flat or gently undulating, characteristic of the Tierra de Pinares region. You'll cycle primarily on tracks and paths through extensive pine forests, agricultural fields, and vineyards. Be aware that some sections, especially after rain, can be sandy and loose, potentially leading to puddles and a more challenging ride. Other forest tracks are easy to navigate.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas are not detailed, you can typically find parking in the towns of Portillo or Mojados to begin your ride. Look for public parking areas within these towns.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery and wildlife can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: You'll be immersed in dense pine forests, including unique ancient stone pine and cork oak stands. Beyond the woodlands, expect traditional Castilian agricultural landscapes and vineyards. The area is rich in wildlife, offering chances to spot small rodents, hares, foxes, and various raptors like the Montagu's harrier. The region is also significant for its wintering crane population.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is generally during the drier seasons to avoid the potentially challenging sandy and muddy sections that can occur after rain. Spring and early autumn often offer pleasant temperatures and more stable trail conditions.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are allowed on trails in this region. However, it's always best to keep them on a leash, especially when passing through agricultural areas, near wildlife, or in towns like Portillo and Mojados, to ensure their safety and respect local regulations.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the "Tierra de Pinares – circular desde Portillo por Mojados" route. It is freely accessible.

faqs.question: What notable landmarks or points of interest can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes by several interesting spots. You can see the historic Portillo Castle, the beautiful San Juan Church in Mojados, and the scenic Megeces Bridge.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other official cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other official paths. You'll find yourself on parts of trails such as Vuelta a San Marugán, Senda del Cega, and Por Campiñas y Humedales, among others, for short stretches.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as moderate. While much of it is relatively flat, the presence of sandy sections, particularly after rain, can increase the technical challenge and require a good level of physical fitness for touring cyclists.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food, drinks, or accommodation in Portillo or Mojados?
faqs.answer: Yes, both Portillo and Mojados are towns where you can find amenities. You should be able to find local restaurants, cafes, and potentially accommodation options to refuel or stay overnight before or after your ride.
